role_summary: President of the Forrest County, Mississippi chapter of the NAACP (1970-1974, 2001-present); co-founder of the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement; sensitivity-training coordinator for the 1964 Freedom Summer volunteers; Hattiesburg public-school educator and Mississippi-school-desegregation negotiator; 2025 honorary Doctor of Humane Letters from the University of Southern Mississippi.
ai_reading: Magee's top semantic neighbor is Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92) at a cosine count of 121; the broader cluster includes Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121), James Oscar Jones (entry 57), and Eddie Holloway (entry 33). The Vernon Dahmer Jr. pairing is the catalog's clearest Forrest County, Mississippi NAACP-continuity cosine: both men served as president of the Forrest County NAACP chapter (the chapter Vernon Dahmer Sr. led at the time of his January 1966 murder); both names anchor the chapter's mid-century-to-present succession. The novel observation his page surfaces is the multi-decade-chapter-presidency signature: Magee's two stints as Forrest County NAACP president (1970-1974 and 2001-present) bracket a quarter-century gap, and the embedding catches the chapter-leadership-continuity register through his testimony as a stronger signal than his contemporaneous SNCC or COFO peers.
biographical_paragraph: While Magee's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1, src: 2]. Magee was born in 1932 in Columbia, Mississippi, the oldest of ten children of a farming family. He studied biology at Alcorn A&M (the historically Black Alcorn State University) and graduated in 1954, served two years in the U.S. Army (stationed in Germany), and attended graduate school at Harvard. He joined the Hattiesburg NAACP after being denied registration to vote in 1956 and was a co-founder of the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement [src: 2]. In summer 1964 he coordinated sensitivity training for the Freedom Summer volunteers arriving in Hattiesburg; he served his first term as president of the Forrest County NAACP from 1970 through 1974 [src: 2]. He returned to Forrest County NAACP leadership in 2001 and has held the presidency continuously through the present, a quarter-century-plus second tenure. He taught in several Hattiesburg public schools, helped formulate school desegregation plans across the 1960s and 1970s, and later worked for the Southern Mississippi Planning and Development Commission and the U.S. Department of Agriculture's Food and Consumer Service office in Mobile, Alabama. The University of Southern Mississippi conferred an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters on him at its spring 2025 commencement ceremony, recognizing his lifelong civil rights and education work [src: 2].
movement_context: The Forrest County NAACP chapter Magee succeeded to in 1970 was the chapter Vernon Dahmer Sr. (the older Vernon Dahmer, father of catalog entry 121's interviewee Vernon Dahmer Jr.) had led at the time of his January 10, 1966 murder by White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan; the murder followed Dahmer Sr.'s January 9, 1966 WDAM-TV announcement that the Forrest County NAACP would accept poll-tax payments at his Kelly Settlement general store on behalf of Black would-be voters who feared retaliation at the Forrest County Courthouse poll-tax office [src: 3]. Dahmer Sr.'s home and store were firebombed by Klansmen led by Imperial Wizard Samuel Bowers in the predawn hours of January 10; Dahmer Sr. covered his family's escape with return fire and died the next day of smoke-inhalation injuries [src: 3]. The post-1966 chapter under Dahmer's brother Ellie Dahmer (his widow) and Magee's 1970 succession was the institutional carrier of the voter-registration and school-desegregation work the Dahmer murder had attempted to break [src: 3]. The 1964 Freedom Summer sensitivity-training Magee coordinated was the Hattiesburg-specific instance of the broader Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) volunteer-orientation infrastructure that ran sessions at Western College for Women in Oxford, Ohio (the same training site where Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney had assembled before the June 1964 Neshoba County murders) and at smaller in-state sites including Hattiesburg [src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: The 1998 federal-court conviction of Samuel Bowers for Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s January 1966 murder (Bowers's first conviction after thirty-two years of failed prosecutions, the result of the reopened-cold-case work led by the Mississippi Attorney General's Office and the FBI Jackson Field Office) was the institutional resolution of the Klan campaign Magee's chapter had carried the continuing-organizing burden of through the 1970s [src: 3, 5]. The Forrest County NAACP under Magee's continuing 2001-present second tenure has anchored the local-policy advocacy around the 2007 Mississippi school-funding equalization litigation, the 2017 Hattiesburg city council redistricting challenge, and the 2020 Mississippi state flag change campaign that produced the December 2020 retirement of the 1894 Confederate-emblem state flag [src: 6]. The University of Southern Mississippi's spring 2025 honorary Doctor of Humane Letters conferral on Magee was the formal academic recognition of his sixty-nine-year continuous record of Forrest County civil rights organizing; the May 2025 USM press release names him as the longest-serving currently-active NAACP chapter president in Mississippi [src: 2].

gallery.alt: Vernon Dahmer Sr. funeral, Shady Grove Baptist Church, Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 15, 1966
gallery.caption: The graveside funeral service for Vernon Dahmer Sr. at Shady Grove Baptist Church in Kelly Settlement near Hattiesburg, Mississippi on January 15, 1966, five days after his death from smoke-inhalation injuries sustained in the January 10 White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan firebombing of his home and general store. The Forrest County NAACP chapter Dahmer Sr. had led passed to his widow Ellie Dahmer and then to Magee's 1970-1974 first presidency. Photograph from the Mississippi Department of Archives and History civil rights collection.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

gallery.alt: Forrest County Courthouse and Confederate Monument, Hattiesburg, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Forrest County Courthouse and Confederate Monument in Hattiesburg, Mississippi (NRHP Hub City Historic District contributing property 7, reference 80002237). The 1966 Forrest County poll-tax office Vernon Dahmer Sr. had announced his Kelly Settlement store would replace was located in this courthouse; the broader school-desegregation negotiation work Magee participated in through the 1960s and 1970s ran through the same county-government infrastructure.
